attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old man presents to the Day Surgery Unit for an elective hydrocele repair. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He is a non-smoker and exercises regularly. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His body mass index is 24 kg/m2. He is fully mobile and has no history of venous thromboembolism.

Intra-operative pneumatic compression devices will be applied.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following additional measures would be most appropriate for venous thromboembolism prophylaxis?
options: Graduated compression stockings and enoxaparin until discharge
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a low-risk patient undergoing minor day surgery. Risk assessment for venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis should consider both patient-specific and procedure-specific factors. In this case, the patient has no significant risk factors (young age, normal BMI, non-smoker, no medical conditions, fully mobile) and is undergoing a minor procedure.

No additional measures beyond intraoperative pneumatic compression devices is appropriate for this patient. The procedure is minor, of short duration, and the patient will be mobilising soon after surgery. The risk of VTE in this setting is very low.

Sequential calf compression stockings would be unnecessary given that pneumatic compression devices are already being used intraoperatively. They would not provide additional benefit in this low-risk setting.

Postoperative enoxaparin (chemical prophylaxis) would be excessive for this case. It is typically reserved for patients with multiple risk factors or those undergoing major surgery with prolonged immobilisation.

Postoperative aspirin is not recommended as primary VTE prophylaxis in surgical patients. While it has some role in specific orthopaedic procedures, it is not indicated for minor day surgery.

Graduated compression stockings and enoxaparin would represent significant over-treatment for this low-risk patient. Combined mechanical and chemical prophylaxis is typically reserved for high-risk patients undergoing major surgery.

The key principle is that VTE prophylaxis should be proportional to risk. In low-risk day surgery patients without additional risk factors, mechanical prophylaxis during the procedure is sufficient. Chemical prophylaxis carries its own risks (bleeding, bruising) and should be reserved for situations where the benefits clearly outweigh these risks.
learningPoints: Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis should be tailored to individual patient risk factors and procedure complexity.
For low-risk day surgery patients without additional risk factors, mechanical prophylaxis during surgery is sufficient.
Chemical thromboprophylaxis is not routinely indicated for minor day surgery procedures in low-risk patients.
references: Pannucci CJ, Swistun L, MacDonald JK, Henke PK, Brooke BS. Individualized Venous Thromboembolism Risk Stratification Using the 2005 Caprini Score to Identify the Benefits and Harms of Chemoprophylaxis in Surgical Patients: A Meta-analysis. Ann Surg. 2017 Jun;265(6):1094-1103. doi: 10.1097/SLA.0000000000002126. PMID: 28106607

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
